sanskrit:
अर्जुन उवाच |
निमित्तानि च पश्यामि विपरीतानि केशव |
न च श्रेयोऽनुपश्यामि हत्वा स्वजनमाहवे ॥३१॥
iast:
arjuna uvāca |
nimittāni ca paśyāmi viparītāni keśava |
na ca śreyo'nupaśyāmi hatvā svajanamāhave ||31||
explanation: Arjuna says to Krishna: 'I see only evil omens, O Keshava. I cannot see any good in slaying my own kinsmen in this battle.' This marks Arjuna's shift from observation to emotional crisis — he now reads the external world as confirming his inner dread.
synthesis: Arjuna's vision of evil omens reveals how deeply subjective perception becomes when fear takes hold. The Advaita tradition sees this as the mind's projection — a consciousness clouded by attachment reads neutral events as catastrophic signs. Ramanujacharya would note that Arjuna's love for his kinsmen is genuine but misdirected — attachment to particular bodies obscures the soul's eternal nature. From the Bhakti perspective, Arjuna's crisis is the necessary precondition for surrender: the ego must exhaust itself before grace can enter. Kashmir Shaivism reads the omens as Shiva's play — Spanda, the divine pulse, working through apparent chaos toward liberation. Tilak's karma-yoga lens sees a warrior rationalizing inaction through superstition. Vivekananda would say that strength, not omen-reading, is the mark of genuine spirituality. The omens Arjuna sees are real to him — and that psychological reality is the starting point for the Gita's entire teaching.

commentaries.text: Arjuna sees evil omens because his mind is already colored by attachment. The Advaita teaching is clear: the outer world mirrors inner states. When moha (delusion) grips the intellect, the world appears full of inauspicious signs. These omens are projections of Arjuna's own grief, not objective reality.

Arjuna's perception of evil omens reflects genuine sensitivity but misplaced application. The Vishishtadvaita view holds that the Lord arranges all circumstances toward the good of the devotee. Arjuna's error is interpreting events through bodily identification rather than the soul's eternal welfare.

Madhvacharya notes that Arjuna's observation of omens is not wrong in itself — the scriptures acknowledge such signs. His error lies in using them as grounds for abandoning righteous duty. Real spiritual discernment reads signs in light of dharma, not against it.

The bhakti tradition sees Arjuna's despair as the fertile ground from which devotion grows. When the ego's confidence collapses and all worldly signs appear dark, the soul is readied for total surrender to the Divine. The omens Arjuna fears are, paradoxically, auspicious signs of his approaching liberation.

Abhinavagupta reads the evil omens as the contraction of consciousness — anava mala operating at full force. Arjuna's awareness has narrowed to the individual body-mind, unable to perceive the universal Spanda pulsing beneath apparent chaos. The omens are real as experience; unreal as ultimate markers of outcome.

From the Shuddhadvaita perspective, Arjuna's vision of ill omens reflects his temporary separation from the joy of divine love. When the soul is not resting in Krishna's grace, the world appears threatening and bleak. Reunion with divine love transforms perception itself.

Tilak observes that Arjuna is rationalizing inaction by reading omens. A karma-yogi acts according to duty without being swayed by superstitious signs. The Gita consistently points toward action performed in accordance with dharma — not paralysis dressed up as spiritual sensitivity.

Vivekananda would note that strong souls do not wait for external signs to act. Reading omens is a symptom of weakness — the fearful mind seeks external permission to avoid difficult duty. The Gita calls us to the strength that needs no omen because it acts from the clarity of the Self.

applications.personal_growth: When facing a hard decision, notice how quickly the fearful mind finds 'signs' to justify avoiding it. The omens you see often reflect your inner state more than external reality.
applications.career_business: Before a major professional move, anxiety can make every small setback look like a warning. Distinguish between genuine red flags based on data and fear-driven omen-reading that rationalizes inaction.
applications.health: When anxious about a health challenge, the mind can catastrophize — interpreting every symptom as confirmation of the worst outcome. Ground yourself in facts rather than fear-colored perception.
applications.relationships: When a relationship enters difficulty, fear reads every interaction as a bad sign. Stepping back from catastrophizing and looking at the whole picture is essential before making irreversible decisions.

sanskrit:
न काङ्क्षे विजयं कृष्ण न च राज्यं सुखानि च |
किं नो राज्येन गोविन्द किं भोगैर्जीवितेन वा ॥३२॥
iast:
na kāṅkṣe vijayaṃ kṛṣṇa na ca rājyaṃ sukhāni ca |
kiṃ no rājyena govinda kiṃ bhogairjīvitena vā ||32||
explanation: Arjuna says: 'I do not desire victory, O Krishna, nor kingdom, nor pleasures. What is a kingdom to us, O Govinda? What are enjoyments or even life itself?' Arjuna has lost all motivation — the very goals he came to achieve now seem worthless.
synthesis: This verse captures one of the most profound psychological states the Gita examines: the collapse of motivation when the cost of achievement becomes visible. Arjuna is not being noble — he is experiencing what modern psychology calls anhedonia, the inability to find value in previously desired outcomes. The Advaita tradition sees this as a potential spiritual opening: when worldly goals lose their grip, the seeker may turn toward the Absolute. Ramanujacharya, however, cautions that renunciation born of grief is not true vairagya — it is emotional collapse masquerading as detachment. Tilak points out that this statement is contradicted by Arjuna's actions: he did want victory when he blew his conch. The question the Gita poses through Arjuna's despair is whether any human goal justifies action that harms those we love — and the answer it builds toward is that it is not the goal but the quality of action that matters.

commentaries.text: Shankaracharya notes that Arjuna's sudden disinterest in kingdom and pleasure, while born of grief, points toward the truth that no worldly object can provide lasting satisfaction. The Advaita teaching uses this collapse of desire as an entry point: if these things do not bring lasting joy, what does? The answer is Brahman alone.

Ramanuja distinguishes between genuine vairagya (dispassion) arising from discrimination and the grief-induced rejection of life that Arjuna expresses here. True renunciation sees the Lord as the source of all, including legitimate duty. Arjuna's rejection is not elevated detachment but the despair of one who has forgotten that the Lord's will underlies all righteous endeavor.

Madhvacharya reads Arjuna's question — 'What use is kingdom or life?' — as a sincere but misguided inquiry. The purpose of life, in the Dvaita view, is to serve the Lord through righteous action. Abandoning dharma does not honor life; it wastes it. Kingdom and pleasure are not the goal, but the duty attached to them is real.

The bhakti tradition hears in Arjuna's words an unconscious longing for something beyond worldly victory — a longing for Krishna himself. What Arjuna declares he does not want (kingdom, pleasure) reveals what he has always truly wanted but not yet recognized: union with the Divine, which alone satisfies completely.

Abhinavagupta sees Arjuna's exhaustion with desire as the threshold of recognition. When the field of external enjoyments reveals its emptiness, consciousness has an opportunity to turn inward toward its own luminous nature. Arjuna's desirelessness, though grief-born, is structurally similar to the liberating detachment the Kashmir Shaiva masters cultivate intentionally.

Vallabhacharya interprets Arjuna's rejection of worldly goods as the soul's implicit recognition that only Krishna's love is worth having. The grief is an inversion of devotion — the same intensity that could be pure love has turned to suffering because it has not yet found its proper object in the Divine.

Tilak sees this verse as illustrating the danger of results-oriented action. Arjuna wanted victory for the sake of enjoyment; when the cost becomes clear, the goal collapses. The karma-yoga solution is to act without attachment to results — then the loss of a particular outcome does not destroy the will to act.

Vivekananda would say Arjuna has touched a genuine truth — no external achievement brings permanent satisfaction — but has drawn the wrong conclusion. The answer to the emptiness of pleasure is not inaction but the discovery of infinite inner strength. Act from that strength, and victory or defeat ceases to be the point.

applications.personal_growth: When you achieve or are about to achieve something you worked hard for and suddenly feel it is not worth it, pause before concluding the goal was meaningless. The collapse may be grief talking, not wisdom.
applications.career_business: Burnout often manifests as sudden loss of desire for goals that drove you for years. Before making irreversible decisions in this state, distinguish between genuine value reassessment and emotional exhaustion.
applications.health: Depression and grief can strip the meaning from every goal, including health. Recognizing that the loss of motivation is a symptom — not a truth about the world — is the first step toward recovery.
applications.relationships: When you say 'I don't care anymore' during a conflict, check whether that is genuine clarity or emotional shutdown. Decisions made from numbness rarely reflect your deepest values.

sanskrit:
येषामर्थे काङ्क्षितं नो राज्यं भोगाः सुखानि च |
त इमेऽवस्थिता युद्धे प्राणांस्त्यक्त्वा धनानि च ॥३३॥
iast:
yeṣāmarthе kāṅkṣitaṃ no rājyaṃ bhogāḥ sukhāni ca |
ta ime'vasthitā yuddhe prāṇāṃstyaktvā dhanāni ca ||33||
explanation: Arjuna continues: 'Those for whose sake we desire kingdom, enjoyments, and pleasures — they stand here in battle, having renounced their lives and wealth.' The very people Arjuna wanted to win prosperity for are now the opponents he must fight.
synthesis: This verse crystallizes the central tragic irony of the Mahabharata: the war was supposedly fought for the sake of the family, yet the family is destroyed by it. Arjuna articulates what many people discover in the pursuit of success — that the people and relationships that were the original motivation for striving can be casualties of the striving itself. The Advaita tradition reads this as the fundamental contradiction of ego-driven desire: the means destroys the end. Ramanuja would note that this reveals the difference between worldly welfare and spiritual welfare — Krishna is guiding Arjuna to see that the souls of his kinsmen are eternal and cannot be harmed. Tilak's karma-yoga perspective shifts the frame: the welfare of one's family is a legitimate but not ultimate motivation; duty to the social order transcends personal bonds.

commentaries.text: Shankaracharya sees Arjuna's observation as illustrating the self-defeating nature of ego-driven desire. We pursue kingdom 'for them,' yet 'they' are now the obstacle. The Advaita teaching points beyond this paradox: when action is rooted in the Self rather than in attachment to persons, the contradiction dissolves.

Ramanuja notes the profound love behind Arjuna's grief — he genuinely sought the welfare of his kinsmen. But the Vishishtadvaita teaching clarifies that true welfare is the soul's eternal relationship with the Lord, not the preservation of the physical body. Arjuna's care is real but its object is too narrow.

Madhvacharya acknowledges Arjuna's legitimate concern for his family but insists that dharma cannot be compromised even for those we love. The Dvaita view holds that each soul faces its own karma; Arjuna's duty is to act righteously, not to shelter others from the consequences of their own adharma.

The bhakti tradition sees Arjuna's love as beautiful but incomplete — it is love that clings to form rather than to the eternal soul. True devotional love, as embodied by Krishna's own love for all beings, embraces without possessing and serves without attachment to outcomes.

Abhinavagupta reads the paradox — fighting those for whom we fight — as consciousness encountering its own reflection. In Kashmir Shaivism, all apparent others are Shiva's own expression. Arjuna's dilemma is resolved not by choosing sides but by recognizing the one consciousness that animates all warriors on both sides.

Vallabhacharya sees Arjuna's attachment to his kinsmen as natural love that has not yet been offered to Krishna. When all love is redirected toward the Lord, the fear of losing loved ones transforms: one sees every soul as eternally held in divine grace, beyond the reach of any battlefield.

Tilak argues that Arjuna's mistake is using personal relationships as the justification for duty. Karma-yoga teaches that duty must be performed for its own sake, as service to the cosmic order. When personal motives are the only basis for action, their loss paralyzes action.

Vivekananda identifies a universal human trap: we work for others and make them our reason for living, only to discover that they cannot always receive what we sacrifice for them. The solution is to expand the object of love from a few persons to all beings — to serve the Divine in everyone.

applications.personal_growth: When you realize the people you were striving for are not receiving — or cannot receive — what you are sacrificing for them, it is worth asking whether your motivation was truly for them or for your own need to provide.
applications.career_business: Entrepreneurs and executives often justify grueling work by saying it is for their family, only to find the family suffered most from the absence. Examine whether your stated motivations align with your actual choices.
applications.health: Neglecting your own health 'for the family' often ends with the family losing you to illness. The people you are sacrificing for need you present and well, not depleted.
applications.relationships: When you discover that the relationship you were fighting to protect is harmed by the way you fight for it, it is time to change the strategy, not abandon the love.

sanskrit:
आचार्याः पितरः पुत्रास्तथैव च पितामहाः |
मातुलाः श्वशुराः पौत्राः श्यालाः सम्बन्धिनस्तथा ॥३४॥
iast:
ācāryāḥ pitaraḥ putrāstathaiva ca pitāmahāḥ |
mātulāḥ śvaśurāḥ pautrāḥ śyālāḥ sambandhinastathā ||34||
explanation: Arjuna names those standing against him: teachers, fathers, sons, and also grandfathers, maternal uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law, and other relatives. Every category of meaningful relationship is represented on the opposing side.
synthesis: By listing every category of human relationship — teachers, elders, juniors, in-laws, siblings — Arjuna captures the full web of social bonds that give life meaning. The Gita understands that human identity is relational: we know who we are partly through our connections. The Advaita tradition will later reveal that all these are temporary arrangements of the one Self; but before that teaching can land, Arjuna must be seen in his full human reality. Ramanuja honors this relational depth — the Vishishtadvaita tradition sees individuals as nodes in a vast web of divine relationship. The practical teaching is that no human dilemma exists in isolation: every difficult decision touches a web of relationships, and wisdom must account for the full scope of impact.

commentaries.text: Shankaracharya notes that all these relationships — teacher, father, son, in-law — are superimpositions on the one Self. The Advaita teaching does not dismiss them but puts them in perspective: what appears as a web of distinct persons is, at the deepest level, a single consciousness appearing in many forms.

Ramanuja sees Arjuna's enumeration of relationships as spiritually significant. The Vishishtadvaita tradition honors the reality of individual souls and their relationships as genuine expressions of the Lord's own relational nature. Arjuna's grief honors the sacredness of these bonds.

Madhvacharya acknowledges that these relationships represent genuine obligations — not to be dismissed as mere attachment. The Dvaita view recognizes that each relationship carries its own dharma. The question is not whether these bonds matter but whether abandoning righteous duty truly serves them.

The bhakti tradition sees in this list the fullness of worldly love — every kind of human bond named. True devotion does not destroy these loves but offers them all to Krishna, who holds each one with infinite care. The devotee loves all the more deeply by loving through the Divine.

Abhinavagupta reads the list as consciousness cataloguing its own reflections. Each relationship — teacher, son, grandfather — is Shiva appearing in a different mask. The Kashmir Shaiva practitioner cultivates recognition (pratyabhijna) of the one consciousness behind every face on the battlefield.

Vallabhacharya sees in Arjuna's naming of every relationship a heart overflowing with love — love that has not yet found its ultimate home in Krishna. Every person named is a manifestation of the Divine Beloved; when Arjuna comes to see them as such, grief transforms into joy.

Tilak notes that the breadth of relationships Arjuna names shows that this is not a private matter but a social one. The karma-yogi understands that right action must account for the full social fabric — not just personal feeling. The Gita does not ask Arjuna to ignore these bonds but to act rightly within the larger order that encompasses them.

Vivekananda would observe that Arjuna's comprehensive list of relationships shows how thoroughly we define ourselves through others. The Vedantic teaching invites us to find an identity that is not dependent on any particular configuration of relationships — so that we can love freely without the fear that loss of relationship equals loss of self.

applications.personal_growth: Listing all the relationships affected by a difficult decision — as Arjuna does here — is a powerful exercise in honest accountability. It prevents you from pretending the choice only affects you.
applications.career_business: Major organizational decisions affect every stakeholder in a web of relationships. Explicitly mapping that web before deciding ensures you are not blind to the full human cost of your choice.
applications.health: Your health choices affect everyone in your relational web — spouse, children, parents, colleagues. Acknowledging that web can be motivating rather than burdensome: your wellbeing is a gift to those you love.
applications.relationships: When navigating conflict with one person, remember that your relationship exists within a larger web. Extended family, mutual friends, shared communities all carry a stake in how you handle the conflict.

sanskrit:
एतान्न हन्तुमिच्छामि घ्नतोऽपि मधुसूदन |
अपि त्रैलोक्यराज्यस्य हेतोः किं नु महीकृते ॥३५॥
iast:
etānna hantumicchāmi ghnato'pi madhusūdana |
api trailokyarājyasya hetoḥ kiṃ nu mahīkṛte ||35||
explanation: Arjuna declares: 'I do not wish to kill these, O Madhusudana (Krishna), even if they were to kill me — not even for the sovereignty of the three worlds, much less for the sake of the earth.' Arjuna would accept death before he kills his kinsmen.
synthesis: This verse represents the height of Arjuna's moral crisis — and, paradoxically, one of his finest moments as a human being. His refusal to kill even to save his own life, even for limitless reward, reflects a genuine ethical seriousness that the Gita does not simply dismiss. The tradition's response is not 'you are wrong to love them' but 'you are wrong about what harms them.' The Advaita reading sees in Arjuna's absolute refusal an echo of the non-harm (ahimsa) that flows naturally from seeing the Self in all. Ramanuja sees it as love of souls that needs to be elevated from bodily love to soul-love. Tilak sharply notes that this is the sentiment of a warrior abandoning his post — and that the Gita's entire teaching will reconstruct Arjuna's will on a higher foundation. Vivekananda praises the love but calls for the strength that acts rightly even when love makes action costly.

commentaries.text: Arjuna's willingness to die rather than kill reflects, in distorted form, the truth of ahimsa that flows from Self-knowledge. The Advaita sage who sees the Self in all indeed cannot harm another without harming themselves. Arjuna's error is not the feeling but the failure to understand that the Self is beyond death — making his dilemma, at the ultimate level, dissolved by wisdom.

Ramanuja reads Arjuna's declaration as genuine love — and honors it. But the Vishishtadvaita teaching clarifies that love operating from bodily identification protects the wrong thing. The souls of Arjuna's kinsmen are eternal; it is their dharma, not their bodies, that Arjuna should concern himself with. By fighting adharma, he serves their true welfare.

Madhvacharya notes that even the sovereignty of three worlds cannot justify abandoning dharma — and Arjuna rightly understands this. But he applies it wrongly: it is adharma, not the battle itself, that should be refused. Serving righteousness is worth more than any kingdom, which is precisely why Arjuna must fight.

The bhakti tradition finds in Arjuna's words a soul that loves deeply but incompletely. He would give up three worlds for the people he loves — but has not yet offered even this love to Krishna. When love is surrendered to the Divine, it does not diminish; it becomes invincible and clear-sighted.

Abhinavagupta sees Arjuna's absolute refusal as the soul touching the boundary of its own contracted identity. He is, in this moment, fully identified with the web of persons he loves. The Kashmir Shaiva teaching will expand that identity to include all of reality — at which point 'killing' and 'being killed' are both seen as play within the one consciousness.

Vallabhacharya sees Arjuna's total surrender of personal interest — even life and sovereignty — as a rough draft of the complete surrender the devotee makes to Krishna. The love that refuses three worlds for the sake of beloved persons, when redirected toward the Divine, becomes the supreme devotion that the Gita extols.

Tilak is unequivocal: a warrior's duty is to fight when dharma demands it. Arjuna's refusal, however emotionally understandable, is dereliction of duty. The Gita will reconstruct Arjuna's motivation not by eliminating his love but by grounding action in duty rather than in its emotional rewards.

Vivekananda honors the love but not the weakness. To love so much that you refuse to act rightly is not the highest love — it is attachment dressed as nobility. True love is strong enough to act for the beloved's genuine good even when that action is painful. This is the Gita's call to strength.

applications.personal_growth: There are moments when refusing to act — even at cost to yourself — is a genuine expression of values. But it is worth examining whether your refusal is principled or is actually a sophisticated form of avoidance.
applications.career_business: Sometimes the right business decision is one you refuse to make regardless of profit — an ethical line you will not cross. But equally, sometimes non-action disguised as ethics is simply fear of difficult action.
applications.health: Refusing treatment or change because it seems too costly is worth examining closely. Is it wisdom, values, or fear driving the refusal? The stakes — health and life — demand honest self-examination.
applications.relationships: Choosing not to take an action, even a painful one, out of love for those who would be affected is sometimes the right choice. Ensure the love is genuinely for them and not for your own comfort of avoiding conflict.

sanskrit:
निहत्य धार्तराष्ट्रान्नः का प्रीतिः स्याज्जनार्दन |
पापमेवाश्रयेदस्मान्हत्वैतानाततायिनः ॥३६॥
iast:
nihatya dhārtarāṣṭrānnaḥ kā prītiḥ syājjanārdana |
pāpamevāśrayedasmānhatvaitānātatāyinaḥ ||36||
explanation: Arjuna asks: 'What pleasure shall we find in killing the sons of Dhritarashtra, O Janardana? Only sin will accrue to us by slaying these aggressors.' Arjuna now shifts his argument: even if the Kauravas are aggressors (atatayins), killing them still brings sin.
synthesis: This verse shows Arjuna making a sophisticated legal-ethical argument. He acknowledges that the Kauravas are atatayins — aggressors who have committed serious injustices — yet still questions whether killing them is justified. The Advaita tradition will ultimately show that the concept of 'sin' applies only to one who acts from ego-identification; the liberated actor transcends it. Ramanuja holds that sin does not attach to dharmic action performed in surrender to the Lord. Madhvacharya invokes the dharma-shastra directly: killing an aggressor is not only permitted but obligatory for a kshatriya. The philosophical tension Arjuna introduces — can righteous killing still be sinful? — is one that every culture dealing with just war theory must face. The Gita's answer is nuanced: it is not the killing itself but the motivation and identification that determines whether sin accrues.

commentaries.text: Shankaracharya notes that the concept of sin (papa) applies within the realm of action and its fruits. The Advaita teaching transcends this: one who acts from Self-knowledge, without ego-identification as the doer, does not accumulate sin. Arjuna's error is assuming that all action generates karma — it does not, for the one who has realized the actionless Self.

Ramanuja holds that sin cannot accrue from action performed as the Lord's instrument, in service of dharma. The Vishishtadvaita view is that surrender to the Lord purifies action. Arjuna's fear of sin reveals that he is still thinking as an independent agent rather than as a devotee acting in alignment with divine will.

The Dvaita tradition is clear on this point: the dharma-shastra explicitly permits — even requires — the killing of aggressors. Atatayins who have committed grave injustices forfeit certain protections. Arjuna's scruple, while emotionally understandable, contradicts established dharmic law. Sin lies in abandoning duty, not in fulfilling it.

From the bhakti perspective, fear of sin disappears for the devotee who acts entirely for the Lord's sake. The great bhaktas have acted in ways that appeared outwardly transgressive — Prahlada defying his father, the gopis leaving their households — yet incurred no sin because their action was saturated with divine love. Motivation purifies action.

Abhinavagupta sees sin as a category of the contracted individual consciousness — it applies within the framework of limited self-identification. At the level of universal Shiva-consciousness, there is no accumulation of karma in the ordinary sense. The liberated warrior who recognizes the divine play acts without bondage, even in the midst of battle.

Vallabhacharya notes that Krishna himself engages in what appear, by ordinary standards, to be transgressive acts — yet no sin touches him. The devotee who has taken complete refuge in Krishna participates in divine grace; action performed in that refuge is not bound by the ordinary ledger of sin and merit.

Tilak distinguishes between action and its fruits. In karma-yoga, the warrior who fights without ego-ownership of the action does not accumulate the fruits — including sin — of that action. Arjuna's fear of sin is valid for one who acts from ego; it does not apply to one who acts as a pure instrument of duty.

Vivekananda would argue that the fear of incurring sin from necessary action is itself a form of ego — the ego that wants its hands clean even at the cost of abandoning duty. Selfless action for the good of all does not bind. The Gita's call is to act fearlessly in service of what is right.

applications.personal_growth: The fear of being 'the bad person' in a necessary conflict can paralyze us as surely as any other fear. Examine whether your concern is genuine ethical sensitivity or a desire to avoid responsibility.
applications.career_business: Difficult leadership actions — letting people go, ending partnerships, confronting wrongdoing — can feel sinful even when they are right. A clear conscience comes from right motivation and process, not from avoiding hard action entirely.
applications.health: Guilt around self-care — the feeling that prioritizing your own health is selfish or harmful to others — is often a distortion. Examine whether the 'sin' you fear in choosing yourself is real or a story your anxiety tells.
applications.relationships: Setting a firm boundary, ending a harmful relationship, or confronting dishonesty can feel like you are doing something wrong. Distinguishing genuine ethical concern from discomfort with necessary action is essential.

sanskrit:
तस्मान्नार्हा वयं हन्तुं धार्तराष्ट्रान्स्वबान्धवान् |
स्वजनं हि कथं हत्वा सुखिनः स्याम माधव ॥३७॥
iast:
tasmānnārhā vayaṃ hantuṃ dhārtarāṣṭrānsvabāndhavān |
svajanam hi kathaṃ hatvā sukhinaḥ syāma mādhava ||37||
explanation: Arjuna concludes: 'Therefore, we are not justified in killing the sons of Dhritarashtra, our own kinsmen. How can we be happy, O Madhava (Krishna), by killing our own relatives?' He frames it as a question of happiness: killing kin cannot lead to genuine happiness.
synthesis: Arjuna's question — 'How can we be happy having killed our own people?' — is one of the most enduring questions in ethics: can happiness be achieved through means that violate love? The Gita's answer across 18 chapters is that the question itself rests on a misunderstanding of happiness. Worldly happiness (sukha) is always relational and conditional — it depends on circumstances, persons, outcomes. The Gita points toward ananda, the unconditional joy of the Self, which is independent of victory or defeat. The Advaita tradition holds that sukha derived from external causes is always shadowed by the fear of its loss. Ramanuja locates true happiness in the Lord's presence, accessible regardless of circumstance. Tilak shifts the frame: the warrior who acts without attachment does not need victory to produce happiness, and therefore is not paralyzed by the question.

commentaries.text: Shankaracharya notes that Arjuna's question assumes happiness depends on external circumstances — a fundamental misconception. The Advaita teaching is that the source of true joy is the Self (Atman), which is untouched by gain or loss. Killing or not killing does not determine one's deepest happiness; Self-knowledge does.

Ramanuja locates the highest happiness in bhakti — the loving relationship with the Lord that transcends all worldly conditions. A devotee who acts in surrender to Krishna finds a joy that no battlefield outcome can alter. Arjuna's question assumes that happiness comes from circumstance; the Vishishtadvaita teaching reveals it comes from divine communion.

Madhvacharya holds that the happiness of dharmic action is its own reward, independent of outcomes. A kshatriya who fights righteously is fulfilling his God-given nature — and that alignment itself brings a joy that is deeper than the satisfaction of particular results. Abandoning duty brings a deeper unhappiness than any battle.

The bhakti tradition reframes the question entirely: the devotee does not act to be happy but to serve the Lord. When service to Krishna is the motive, the question 'how can we be happy?' dissolves. Joy is the natural overflow of devotion, not the goal of calculation.

Abhinavagupta sees sukha (happiness) as a contracted shadow of the infinite ananda that is the nature of consciousness itself. The practitioner of Kashmir Shaivism discovers that this ananda is always present, not conditioned by whether kinsmen live or die. Arjuna's question assumes a prisoner's understanding of happiness.

Vallabhacharya sees all happiness as flowing from the bliss (ananda) of Krishna. The joy of worldly relationships is real but derivative — it reflects the divine joy that is Krishna's essential nature. When life is lived in relation to Krishna, happiness is available regardless of how circumstances unfold.

Tilak argues that Arjuna's equation of happiness with the survival of kinsmen reveals the fundamental problem with desire-driven action. Karma-yoga decouples action from its fruit — including the fruit of personal happiness. The karma-yogi acts from duty, and whatever happiness follows is a bonus, not the basis.

Vivekananda would challenge the premise: the greatest happiness comes from selfless service, not from the preservation of personal comfort. A life lived in service to what is right — even painfully right — produces a joy that the pleasure-seeker never knows. The soldier who fights for truth has access to a happiness the one who flees duty never finds.

applications.personal_growth: When facing a painful but necessary decision, ask not 'will this make me happy?' but 'will this make me whole?' Happiness is often the wrong metric — integrity, purpose, and meaning go deeper.
applications.career_business: The belief that certain business decisions will destroy morale or happiness is often true in the short term. But the long-term happiness that comes from acting with integrity consistently outlasts the discomfort of difficult decisions.
applications.health: The pursuit of immediate comfort often conflicts with long-term health. True wellbeing sometimes requires accepting present discomfort — in treatment, in lifestyle change — as the price of genuine flourishing.
applications.relationships: The question 'will this make us happy?' is an important one but not the only one. 'Will this be honest, fair, and true to who we are?' is equally important — and the happiness that follows right action is more durable.

sanskrit:
यद्यप्येते न पश्यन्ति लोभोपहतचेतसः |
कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं मित्रद्रोहे च पातकम् ॥३८॥
iast:
yadyapyete na paśyanti lobhopahataсetasaḥ |
kulakṣayakṛtaṃ doṣaṃ mitradrohe ca pātakam ||38||
explanation: Arjuna says: 'Even if these, whose minds are overpowered by greed, do not see the sin of destroying the family and the crime of treachery to friends...' — Arjuna acknowledges the Kauravas' blindness due to greed and begins to set himself apart from their lack of moral awareness.
synthesis: This verse marks a critical and somewhat ironic moment: Arjuna is correctly diagnosing the Kauravas' ethical failure (greed-clouded vision) while being simultaneously blind to how his own crisis is clouding his own vision. The Advaita tradition would note that lobha (greed) and moha (delusion) are both forms of avidya — Arjuna's delusion is different in character from Duryodhana's greed, but both obscure clear perception. The Gita does not present Arjuna as fully morally superior to his opponents here; it presents a human being accurately seeing one flaw while being temporarily blind to another. The practical wisdom is universal: we are most confident in our ethical clarity precisely when we are most in danger of blind spots.

commentaries.text: Shankaracharya notes the irony: Arjuna identifies lobha (greed) as the Kauravas' obscuring factor while moha (delusion) obscures his own vision. Both are forms of avidya — ignorance of the Self. The Advaita teaching sees all these protagonists as minds caught in different expressions of the same fundamental ignorance.

Ramanuja observes that Arjuna's capacity to diagnose the Kauravas' greed-blindness is itself a sign of his spiritual sensitivity. He is not wholly in the wrong — the Kauravas are indeed acting from lobha. But Arjuna's error is concluding that, because they are wrong, he should be inactive. The answer is not inaction but righteous action.

Madhvacharya affirms that greed (lobha) is a genuine spiritual obstruction that prevents clear ethical perception. The Kauravas' inability to see the sin of their actions is a real moral failure. Arjuna sees this clearly. But seeing others' blindness does not exempt one from one's own duty — if anything, it underscores the importance of acting from clarity.

From the bhakti perspective, both greed and grief are obstructions to the clear sight of the soul. The Kauravas are blinded by wanting too much; Arjuna is blinded by fearing loss. The antidote to both is surrender to the Lord, whose vision is unclouded by either desire or grief.

Abhinavagupta reads lobha as a contraction of consciousness — the grasping that narrows awareness to the object of desire. The Kashmir Shaiva path cultivates expansive recognition (pratyabhijna) that sees through both greed and grief as movements within the unlimited field of awareness.

Vallabhacharya sees greed as the antithesis of devotion — the grasping for worldly objects that replaces love of the Divine. The Kauravas' lobha is a spiritual disease; their vision of what is valuable has been corrupted. Arjuna, by contrast, acts from love — however misguided — which is the raw material of devotion.

Tilak notes that Arjuna correctly identifies greed as corrupting judgment and action. The karma-yoga teaching is consistent with this: action driven by personal desire — whether for gain or for comfort — is tainted at its root. Pure action is possible only when desire is subordinated to duty.

Vivekananda would point to the universal truth here: those driven by greed lose the capacity for ethical perception. But he would also caution that the moment we congratulate ourselves on seeing others' blindness, we are vulnerable to our own. Self-knowledge must accompany the judgment of others.

applications.personal_growth: The moment you most clearly see someone else's ethical blindness is often when you are most at risk of missing your own. True self-awareness requires applying the same scrutiny to yourself as to others.
applications.career_business: In competitive environments, it is easy to accurately identify a competitor's or colleague's greed-driven decisions. The harder and more important work is examining whether your own decisions are equally influenced by desire for gain.
applications.health: Recognizing that others are making unhealthy choices driven by short-term gratification is easier than seeing our own. The same critical lens that spots others' unhealthy habits needs to be turned inward regularly.
applications.relationships: Seeing clearly how a partner's or family member's desire or insecurity is distorting their behavior is valuable. But it requires equal honesty about how your own emotional state might be distorting your perception of them.
